notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary officer and Wyoming Valley pioneer

narrative_blocks.header: He held Mud Fort against the British fleet
narrative_blocks.body: In the autumn of 1777 Captain Simon Spalding commanded the Delaware river defenses at Mud Fort below Philadelphia, enduring weeks of bombardment from the British fleet. He had already wintered at Valley Forge and fought at Germantown and Brandywine.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded Sheshequin after the war
narrative_blocks.body: Following his service under Sullivan against the Iroquois, Spalding led the first permanent settlement at Sheshequin on the Susquehanna in 1783. A sixth-generation descendant of Edward Spalding of Massachusetts Bay, he had carried his family out of Connecticut into the contested Wyoming Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons fired on each other's flags
narrative_blocks.body: Spalding's blood spread far across a dividing nation: among his grandsons, two brothers took opposite sides in the Civil War, one fighting for the Union and one for the Confederacy. The general himself had helped settle a frontier his descendants would scatter from Texas to Illinois.

cross_connections.display_label: Wyoming Valley settler and fellow Sullivan campaigner
Wyoming Valley patriot alongside Dr. William Hooker Smith

military_service.unit: Capt. Samuel Ransom's Company; 1st Connecticut Regiment
military_service.notes: Valley Forge, Germantown, and Brandywine; commanded the Delaware river defenses at Mud Fort, 1777; Sullivan Expedition, 1779. DAR Patriot A107688.

military_service.unit: Capt. Samuel Ransom's Company; 1st Connecticut Regiment
military_service.notes: Valley Forge, Germantown, and Brandywine; commanded the Delaware river defenses at Mud Fort, 1777; Sullivan Expedition, 1779. DAR Patriot A107688.

narrative_blocks.header: A teenage fifer on Sullivan's brutal campaign
narrative_blocks.body: At fourteen John Spalding fifed for his father's company on the 1779 Sullivan Expedition against the Iroquois, encamped at Fort Sullivan in present-day Athens. He was the eldest son of General Simon Spalding, who founded the first settlement at Sheshequin.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose from fifer to colonel of militia
narrative_blocks.body: The boy who had piped his father's men into the wilderness was later commissioned colonel of the Pennsylvania state militia. He settled at Sheshequin on the Susquehanna, where he and Wealthy Ann Gore raised fourteen children.

cross_connections.display_label: shared the Wyoming Valley frontier with judge Matthias Hollenback
Wyoming Valley soldier alongside Dr. William Hooker Smith

bio.title: Col.
bio.bio_blurb: Revolutionary fifer and Sheshequin militia colonel

military_service.notes: Fifer in his father Capt. Simon Spalding's company; accompanied Gen. Sullivan's 1779 expedition through the Susquehanna and Chemung valleys; later commissioned colonel in the Pennsylvania state militia.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was Sheshequin's Judge Gore
narrative_blocks.body: Wealthy Ann was a daughter of Judge Obadiah Gore, a Wyoming Valley pioneer whose family was shattered at the 1778 Battle of Wyoming. He built Sheshequin's first framed house, store, grist-mill, and distillery and sat for years as an associate judge of the county court.

bio.bio_blurb: Susquehanna frontier settler of Sheshequin, Pennsylvania
